content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="frequens_vester_67_1">
								, <gloss>the sight of you in full assembly</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_locus_67_2">,
								the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Rostra</foreign>. The scanty remains of
								the <foreign xml:lang="lat">rostra</foreign> may be seen at the left of the
								Temple of Concord. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_agendum_67_2">
								, <gloss>for public business</gloss>, i.e. among the many duties of
								a magistrate there is none more dignified (<foreign xml:lang="lat">
								amplissimus</foreign>) than this of addressing the whole people in a
								political assembly; <foreign xml:lang="lat">agere
									cum
									populo</foreign> was the technical expression for transacting
								business in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia</foreign> or a <foreign xml:lang="lat">contio</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ornatissimus_67_3">
								, <gloss>honorable</gloss> (of private glory as an orator). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Quirites_67_3">
								, <gloss>fellow-citizens</gloss>: the name by which the Romans were
								addressed when acting in a civil capacity. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_aditu_67_3">
								, <gloss>this avenue</gloss> (i.e. addressing the people on
								political questions). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="optimo_cuique_67_4">,
								i.e. to such as the presiding magistrate would permit, for only
								these had a right to speak in a <foreign xml:lang="lat">contio</foreign>
								. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rationes_67_5">
								, <gloss>plan</gloss>: the plural indicates the details of the plan,
								i.e. the particular considerations that determine a general course
								of conduct. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_68_1">
								(temporal), <gloss>while</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 546">546</ref>
								(323, 2); B. 288, I, b; G. 585; H. 600, ii, I (521, 2); H.-B. 524. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritatem_68_2">:
								the act of speaking in a <foreign xml:lang="lat">contio</foreign>
								indicated that the speaker was a proper person to advise the people,
								and hence it would confer <foreign xml:lang="lat">auctoritas</foreign>
								( <gloss>
									weight</gloss>, <gloss>
									prestige</gloss>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="attingere_68_2">
								, <gloss>aspire to</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perfectum_ingenio_68_3">
								, <gloss>perfected by force of intellect</gloss>, i.e. the fruit of
								fully developed mental power. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="elaboratum_68_4">
								, <gloss>carefully wrought</gloss> (such, therefore, as needed more
								practice than youth could give). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amicorum_temporibus_68_5">
								, <gloss>exigencies of my friends</gloss>. A Roman lawyer was not
								regarded as doing a service for hire, but was expected to defend his
								friends gratuitously. He was, indeed, prohibited from receiving pay;
								but, though no bargain was made, the obliged party was expected to
								give a liberal present, in some form or other, to his <foreign xml:lang="lat">patronus</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_et_68_5">:
								here the first clause is virtually concessive; we may render <gloss>while
								. . . at the same time</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="caste_68_7">
								, <gloss>with
									clean hands</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="integre_68_7">
								(next line), <gloss>in good faith</gloss> (toward the client). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicio_68_8">,
								i.e. their action in electing him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fructum_68_8">,
								i.e. the several grades of office he had already filled: he was now <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dilationem_68_9">
								, <gloss>adjournment</gloss> There were many things which could
								break up an assembly and put off the business, especially
								unfavorable auguries, the announcement of which was a favorite
								device of politicians. If an election was thus interrupted by
								adjournment, the votes already taken were null and void and the
								whole proceeding had to be gone through with again. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">comitia</foreign> at which Cicero was chosen <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> were twice adjourned in this
								way, so that there were three meetings before the election was
								complete. At each of these Cicero was the first (<foreign xml:lang="lat">primus</foreign>) of the eight praetors to secure
								a majority, and hence he was <gloss>thrice declared elected</gloss>
								( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ter renuntiatus sum</foreign>). <foreign xml:lang="lat">primus</foreign> does not here imply a
								superiority in rank, for the eight praetors were regarded as
								colleagues and they determined their functions by lot. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_aliis_68_11">,
								etc., i.e. this action of the voters showed that they approved his
								course of life, and was a suggestion to others how to attain similar
								honors. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_68_12">,
								opposed to the time referred to in sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 1">1</ref>
								. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_agendum_68_14">,
								for <gloss>speaking</gloss> (cf. note on 1.2, above). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="forensi_usu_68_15">:
								the courts were held in the Forum. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quoque_68_18">,
								i.e. to forensic as well as to military or political activity. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_68_19">
								(the strongest of the <gloss>and's</gloss>), <gloss>and
									further</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_68_19">
								(nom.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="laetandum_68_19">:
								the construction <foreign xml:lang="lat">illud
									laetor</foreign> changed to the passive; § <ref n="AG 390">390</ref>,
								c (238, b); cf. B. 176, 2; G. 333, I, N.1; H. 405 (371, iii); H.-B.
								397, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mihi_68_20">,
								following <foreign xml:lang="lat">insolita</foreign>. <pb n="332"/>
								</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ratione_68_21">
								, <gloss>
									manner</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oratio_68_22">
								, <gloss>
									language</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="orationis_68_22">
								(1.24), <gloss>argument</gloss> (abstract from <foreign xml:lang="lat">oro</foreign>, in its original sense of <gloss>to
								speak</gloss>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="virtute_68_23">
								, <gloss>good
									qualities</gloss> generally. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_68_27">
								(cf. note on 1.19, above), <gloss>and now</gloss> (to come to the
								point). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inde_68_27">
								, <gloss>from
									that point</gloss>
								</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vectigalibus_sociis_68_29">
								, <gloss>tributaries and allies</gloss> (of the latter some were
								tributary and others not). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="relictus_68_30">,
								i.e. before the contest was fully decided. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lacessitus_68_30">
								(next line), <gloss>am assailed</gloss>, not seriously attacked. By
								using these words Cicero artfully prepares for the assertion which
								he is about to make of the need of an energetic commander. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Asiam_68_31">,
								i.e. the province of this name, occupying the western half of Asia
								Minor and bordering on the dominions of Mithridates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equitibus_68_32">,
								etc.: keep the emphasis by changing the construction: <gloss>Roman <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> are daily receiving</gloss>,
								etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quorum_occupatae_68_33">
								, <gloss>whose large properties, invested in managing your
									revenues, are endangered</gloss>. The revenues were farmed out
								to <foreign xml:lang="lat">societates</foreign> (companies) of <foreign xml:lang="lat">publicani</foreign>, who were members of the
								equestrian order (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 14">14</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necessitudine_69_2">:
								Cicero was of an equestrian family. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Bithyniae_69_4">:
								this territory had been bequeathed to the Roman republic by
								Nicomedes III, <date when="-0074">B.C. 74</date>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ariobarzanis_69_5">:
								king of Cappadocia, which had been overrun by Mithridates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Lucullum_69_7">:
								Lucullus was related to both branches of the family of Metellus, and
								had married Clodia, sister of the notorious Publius Clodius. It was
								chiefly this mischievous demagogue, who was serving as one of his
								officers, that stirred up the dissensions and mutinies which robbed
								Lucullus of the fruits of his victories. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="discedere_69_7">
								, <gloss>is
									on the point of withdrawing</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huic_successerit_69_7">
								, <gloss>his successor</gloss>, Glabrio. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_paratum_69_8">
								, <gloss>not adequately furnished</gloss>—an understatement: Glabrio
								had shown himself thoroughly incompetent, but Cicero was on good
								terms with him. This was the Glabrio who had presided over the court
								in the case of Verres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="socus_69_9">,
								i.e. Asiatics. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civibus_69_9">,
								Romans engaged in business in Asia. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imperatorem_69_10">
								(in pred. appos. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">unum</foreign>), <gloss>as
								commander</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agitur_69_17">
								, <gloss>is
									at stake</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="certissima_69_21">
								: <gloss>the
									surest</gloss> because Asia was the richest and most fruitful of
								all the provinces; hence the price paid by the <foreign xml:lang="lat">publicani</foreign> for the privilege of farming
								its taxes was always certain to be large. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_amissis_69_22">:
								equiv. to a fut. <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ornamenta_69_22">
								, <gloss>
									ornaments</gloss>, i.e. "all that exalts and embellishes
								civilized life." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_vobis_70_1">:
								the abl. with <foreign xml:lang="lat">a</foreign> is used instead of
								the dat. of agent because there is another dat. dependent on <foreign xml:lang="lat">
									consulendum</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 374">374</ref>, N.1 (232,
								N.); B. 189,1, a; G. 355, R.; H. 431, I (388, N.); H-B. 373, I, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civis_Romanos_70_8">,
								etc.: this massacre (<date when="-0088">B.C. 88</date>), in which
								80,000 persons perished, was intended by Mithridates as a step
								toward the entire expulsion of the Romans from Asia. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="regnat_70_11">:
								for tense, see § <ref n="AG 466">466</ref>(276, a); B. 259, 4; G.
								230; H. 533 (467, 2); H-B. 485. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etenim_70_14">
								, <gloss>
									for</gloss> (you will notice). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="triumphavit_de_70_17">:
								not <gloss>triumphed over</gloss>, but <gloss>celebrated a
									triumph for a victory over</gloss>. The word is repeated in
								emphatic antithesis to the clause <foreign xml:lang="lat">sed</foreign>
								. . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">regnaret</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="regnaret_70_19">
								, <gloss>was
									still a king</gloss> (i.e. in possession of his kingdom). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="verum_tamen_70_19">
								, <gloss>but
									still</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_egerunt_70_20">
								, <gloss>for
									what they have done</gloss>: by a Latin idiom quod is here
								equivalent to <foreign xml:lang="lat">propter id quod</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="res_publica_70_22">
								, <gloss>the
									public: interest</gloss> Sulla had hastened to make an
								unsatisfactory peace, that he might return and restore order in
								Italy, which was in the power of the Marian faction. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="autem_70_23">
								, <gloss>
									now</gloss> (in contrast to the action of the Roman generals). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reliquum_70_23">
								, <gloss>that
									followed</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Bosporanis_70_27">
								, <gloss>the
									people of Bosporus</gloss>, a flourishing Grecian state,
								embracing the Crimea and adjoining lands. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_duces_70_29">,
								i.e. Sertorius and his comrades. Sertorius was the ablest general of
								the Marian faction in the Civil Wars. After the victory of Sulla,
								and the complete overthrow of his own party elsewhere, he continued
								to hold Spain, where he attempted to found a new republic, entering
								into alliance with Mithridates and other enemies of Rome. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gereretur_71_1">
								(for fut. indic.): subj. of integral part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_imperio_71_2">
								, <gloss>for
									supremacy</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="alterius_altera_71_3">,
								1.6, below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="firmamenti_71_4">
								, <gloss>outward
									support</gloss>
								</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="roboris_71_4">
								, <gloss>internal
									strength</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Cn_Pompei_71_5">:
								Pompey and Metellus Pius conducted the war against Sertorius from <date when="-0077">B.C. 77</date> till <date when="-0072">B.C.
									72</date> without being able to subdue him. In 72 Sertorius was
								assassinated by his lieutenant Perperna, whom Pompey had no
								difficulty in defeating. Cicero, it will be observed, suppresses
								these details, preferring to give Pompey credit, in general terms,
								for putting an end to "the danger from Sertorius." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_parte_71_6">,
								i.e. in the East. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="felicitati_71_8">:
								observe the chiastic order of the ideas, — <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								felicitati</foreign>
									<foreign xml:lang="lat">virtuti</foreign> ; <foreign xml:lang="lat">culpae, fortunae</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_extrema_71_8">
								(an intentional euphemism), <gloss>these late disasters</gloss>, <pb n="335"/>
								</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tribuenda_71_9">
								, <gloss>
									attributable</gloss>. In fact the ill success of Lucullus was in
								great part due to the machinations of politicians at Rome; he was
								not properly supported by the home government. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mercatoribus_71_18">,
								etc.: abl. abs. expressing cause. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="appellati_71_19">
								, <gloss>
									addressed</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="superbius_71_19">
								, <gloss>too
									haughtily</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legatum_71_21">,
								etc.: M'. Aquilius, the person referred to, had in fact forfeited
								all claim to the inviolability of an ambassador by actually taking
								command of an army against Mithridates. He was taken prisoner and
								put to death (<date when="-0088">B.C.
									88</date>). Aquilius had done service to the state by
								suppressing the Servile War in Sicily. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videte_ne_71_26">:
								the Latin expresses in the form of a purpose clause ("see to it
								lest," etc.) what we should put in the form of an indir. quest.
								("see whether it be not," etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_71_27">
								, <gloss>
									as</gloss>, correl. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sic_71_27">
								. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illis_71_27">,
								i.e. your ancestors. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_posse_71_29">:
								subject of sit. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_71_30">
								a regular formula of transition, <gloss>again</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_71_30">
								, <gloss>
									that:</gloss> § <ref n="AG 572">572</ref>, b (333, b); G. 542;
								H. 588, 3 (540, iv, N.); H.-B. 552, 1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="periculum_discrimen_71_30">
								, <gloss>a dangerous crisis</gloss>: the former word signifying the <gloss>
								trial</gloss>; the latter the <gloss>decision</gloss>. (See Introd.,
								p. xlv.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ariobarzanes_72_1">:
								king of Cappadocia. It was the designs of Mithridates upon this
								kingdom that first brought him into collision with Rome. (Fig. 20 is
								from a fine bust of some unknown Cappadocian.) <pb n="336"/>
								</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="certum_72_6">
								, <gloss>a
									particular</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_72_6">:
								causal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sine_periculo_72_7">,
								i.e. by offending Lucullus and Glabrio. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="propter_72_10">
								, <gloss>at

								if he did not mean to talk about the ordinary <foreign xml:lang="lat">virtutes</foreign> recognized as necessary for a
								general, but intended to speak of certain others, equally necessary
								but perhaps less common (incorruptibility, etc.), for which Pompey
								was eminent. But he goes on at once to emphasize the possession of
								the commonly recognized soldierly qualities by Pompey, as if he had
								forgotten his point in his enthusiasm. Then, with sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 36">36</ref>, he suddenly pulls himself up, as
								from a digression, and returns to consider the good qualities he
								had, as he says, "begun to enumerate." By this method, not only is
								an air of spontaneity given to the praise of Pompey (as if the
								orator were carried away by his theme; cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 3">3</ref>), but the special and rare virtues on
								which he wishes to lay stress are much emphasized by being, as it
								were, brought in twice, — a second time when the orator seems in
								danger of forgetting them (sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 36">36</ref>). </gloss>
